title: On the Feasibility of Low-Cost Wearable Sensors for Multi-Modal Biometric Verification

method.input_representation: Walsh–Hadamard and Fourier transform coefficients (96 per signal) and statistical metrics for GSR
method.training_strategy: Density estimation using mean and covariance matrix of the target user's feature vectors

evaluation.context: Achieved in Scenario 3 using ECG, PPG, and GSR with a window size of 10 and 30% training data
Achieved in Scenario 3 using ECG, PPG, and GSR with a window size of 10 and 30% training data

evaluation_protocol: One-class classification; window-based decision combining (summation of outputs); 3 repetitions averaged.

reported_limitations: Physiological signals vary over time due to user state or ageing
Prototype not comfortable for long-term wear

usability.usability_issues: Rudimentary prototype was not comfortable enough to wear over long periods of time

reproducibility.benchmark_used: Private dataset made public via Dropbox

key_contribution: Feasibility analysis of low-cost wearable sensors for multi-modal biometric verification
Provision of a public dataset of PPG, ECG, ACC, and GSR signals
Demonstration that combining ECG, PPG, and GSR significantly outperforms unimodal systems
main_strength: Multimodal approach leveraging multiple physiological and behavioral signals
High transparency through the release of code and data
Evaluation of performance across different user states (sitting vs walking)
main_weakness: No active spoofing or presentation attack testing
insights.notes_for_review: Scenario 3 (training on multiple activities) provided the best results, suggesting that multi-state training is critical for ECG-based wearables.
